docs.title: Ondine
docs.text:
Run LLMs on tabular data — 100x fewer API calls, 40-50% lower cost.

Ondine is an open-source Python SDK for data engineers and ML practitioners who need to process large CSV/DataFrame datasets with LLMs. Multi-row batching, prefix caching, and budget controls are built in from day one — not bolted on later.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
from ondine import QuickPipeline

result = QuickPipeline.create(
    data="products.csv",
    prompt="Classify this product into a category: {name} - {description}",
    model="gpt-4o-mini"
)

docs.title: Why Ondine?
docs.text:
Most teams processing tabular data with LLMs hit the same three walls: API cost spirals, brittle pipelines that crash halfway through, and hallucinated outputs that are hard to catch at scale. Ondine is built to solve exactly those three problems.

Problem Ondine's answer API bills too high Multi-row batching (100 rows/call) + prefix caching → 40-50% savings Pipeline crashes lose progress Checkpointing — resume from last saved row, not row 0 LLM makes things up Context Store — ground each response against your source data Too many providers to juggle 100+ providers via LiteLLM, single unified API

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Quick API — 3-line hello world with smart defaults
  • Builder API — Full control over pipeline configuration
  • 100+ LLM providers via LiteLLM (OpenAI, Anthropic, Groq, Azure, local MLX)
  • Multi-row batching — Process 100 rows per API call
  • Prefix caching — 40-50% cost reduction on repeated prompts
  • Cost estimation — Know the cost before you run
  • Budget limits — Hard caps on spending
  • Checkpointing — Resume failed pipelines from where they stopped
  • Structured output — Pydantic models, JSON, regex parsing
  • Observability — OpenTelemetry, Langfuse, structured logging

docs.title: Contributing to Ondine
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Ondine!

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
1. Fork and Clone

Click Fork on github.com/ptimizeroracle/ondine, then clone your fork: 

git clone https://github.com/ptimizeroracle/ondine.git
cd ondine
git remote add upstream https://github.com/ptimizeroracle/ondine.git

  1. Set Up Development Environment 

    # Install uv (if not already installed)
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Install dependencies
uv sync --extra dev --extra observability

# Install pre-commit hooks
uv run pre-commit install

  2. Create a Branch 

    git checkout -b feature/your-feature-name

docs.title: Style
docs.text:
• Follow PEP 8 and the Zen of Python
  • Use type hints for all function signatures
  • Keep functions small and focused (KISS principle)
  • Write self-documenting code with clear variable names

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• Write tests for all new features (TDD encouraged)
  • Maintain or improve test coverage (currently 95%+)
  • Include both unit and integration tests where appropriate
  • Use descriptive test names: test_<what>_<when>_<expected>

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Update README.md if adding user-facing features
  • Add docstrings to all public functions and classes
  • Include examples for new features in examples/ directory
  • Do not manually bump package versions in pyproject.toml or ondine/__init__.py
  • Do not manually add release entries unless a maintainer asks; release notes are managed through Release Please

docs.title: Commits
docs.text:
• Write clear, descriptive commit messages
  • Use conventional commits format:
  • feat: - New feature
  • fix: - Bug fix
  • docs: - Documentation changes
  • test: - Test additions or changes
  • refactor: - Code refactoring
  • chore: - Maintenance tasks

Example: 

feat: add support for custom retry strategies

- Implement RetryStrategy interface
- Add exponential backoff with jitter
- Update documentation

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Ondine follows a 5-layer clean architecture:

  1. Core - Domain models and business logic
  2. Adapters - External integrations (LLM clients, I/O)
  3. Stages - Pipeline processing stages
  4. Orchestration - Execution strategies and state management
  5. API - Public interfaces (builders, composers)

docs.title: Plugin System
docs.text:
Ondine uses decorators for extensibility:

  • @provider - Register custom LLM providers
  • @stage - Register custom pipeline stages

See examples/15_custom_llm_provider.py and examples/16_custom_pipeline_stage.py for details.

docs.title: Adding a New LLM Provider
docs.text:
1. Create a new class inheriting from BaseLLMProvider
  2. Implement invoke() and estimate_tokens() methods
  3. Register with @provider("your_provider_name")
  4. Add tests in tests/unit/test_providers.py
  5. Add example in examples/
  6. Update README.md

docs.title: Adding a New Pipeline Stage
docs.text:
1. Create a new class inheriting from PipelineStage
  2. Implement execute() method
  3. Register with @stage("your_stage_name")
  4. Add tests in tests/unit/test_stages.py
  5. Add example in examples/
  6. Update documentation

docs.title: Reporting Bugs
docs.text:
1. Check if the bug is already reported in Issues
  2. If not, create a new issue with:
  3. Clear title and description
  4. Steps to reproduce
  5. Expected vs actual behavior
  6. Environment details (OS, Python version, Ondine version)
  7. Minimal reproducible example

docs.title: Suggesting Features
docs.text:
1. Check existing feature requests
  2. Open a new issue with:
  3. Clear use case description
  4. Proposed API or interface
  5. Example usage
  6. Why this would benefit users

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Before submitting:
  2. Ensure all tests pass: just test
  3. Run code quality checks: just check
  4. Update documentation if needed

  5. Use a Conventional Commit style PR title if possible (feat:, fix:, docs:, etc.)

  6. Submit PR:

  7. Write a clear title and description
  8. Reference related issues (e.g., "Fixes #123")
  9. Ensure CI passes (tests, linting, security)

  10. Respond to code review feedback

  11. After approval:

  12. Maintainers will merge your PR
  13. Release Please will prepare the next release from merged changes on main

docs.title: Release Process
docs.text:
Ondine uses GitHub Actions plus Release Please for releases:

  1. PRs merge into main
  2. Release Please updates or opens the release PR
  3. Publishing a GitHub release triggers the PyPI publish workflow

Maintainers should avoid manual version bumps unless they are intentionally repairing release metadata.

docs.title: Core Concepts
docs.text: This page covers the pieces that make up an Ondine pipeline and how they connect. If something breaks, this is the mental model that will help you find the problem.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: Ondine uses a layered architecture:

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
The Pipeline is the central execution unit. It drives data through stages in order.

from ondine import Pipeline

# Pipelines are built via PipelineBuilder
pipeline = PipelineBuilder.create()...build()

# Execute synchronously
result = pipeline.execute()

# Execute asynchronously
result = await pipeline.execute_async()

Once built, a pipeline is immutable and thread-safe. It owns all configuration, handles checkpointing and recovery, and tracks costs and metrics throughout execution.

docs.title: Pipeline Stages
docs.text:
Data flows through composable stages in a fixed order:

  • DataLoaderStage loads from CSV, Excel, Parquet, or DataFrame.
  • PromptFormatterStage interpolates row data into your template.
  • BatchAggregatorStage (optional) packs N prompts into a single API call.
  • LLMInvocationStage calls the LLM with retry and rate limiting.
  • BatchDisaggregatorStage (optional) splits the batch response back into N results.
  • ResponseParserStage parses the LLM output (text, JSON, or regex).
  • ResultWriterStage writes results to the output destination.

The two batch stages only appear when batch_size > 1. Multi-row batching can yield up to 100x fewer API calls, with automatic context-window validation and partial-failure handling.

docs.title: Pipeline Builder
docs.text:
PipelineBuilder exposes a fluent API for assembling pipelines:

from ondine import PipelineBuilder

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    # Data source
    .from_csv("data.csv", input_columns=["text"], output_columns=["result"])

    # Prompt configuration
    .with_prompt("Process: {text}")

    # LLM configuration
    .with_llm(provider="openai", model="gpt-4o-mini")

    # Processing configuration
    .with_batch_size(100)
    .with_concurrency(5)
    .with_retry_policy(max_retries=3)

    # Build immutable pipeline
    .build()
)

Available builder methods by category:

  • Data: from_csv(), from_dataframe(), from_parquet(), from_excel()
  • Prompt: with_prompt(), with_system_prompt()
  • LLM: with_llm(), with_llm_spec()
  • Processing: with_batch_size(), with_concurrency(), with_rate_limit()
  • Reliability: with_retry_policy(), with_checkpoint()
  • Cost: with_max_budget()
  • Execution: with_async_execution(), with_streaming()

docs.title: Custom Stages
docs.text:
You can extend PipelineStage to insert your own processing logic:

from ondine.stages import PipelineStage

class MyCustomStage(PipelineStage):
    def process(self, input_data, context):
        # Your processing logic
        return processed_data

    def validate_input(self, input_data):
        # Validation logic
        return ValidationResult(valid=True)

docs.title: Specifications
docs.text: Configuration lives in Pydantic models. Each spec type covers one concern:

docs.title: DatasetSpec
docs.text:
from ondine.core.specifications import DatasetSpec

spec = DatasetSpec(
    source="data.csv",
    input_columns=["text"],
    output_columns=["result"],
    format="csv"
)

docs.title: PromptSpec
docs.text:
from ondine.core.specifications import PromptSpec

spec = PromptSpec(
    template="Summarize: {text}",
    system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant."
)

docs.title: LLMSpec
docs.text:
from ondine.core.specifications import LLMSpec

spec = LLMSpec(
    provider="openai",
    model="gpt-4o-mini",
    temperature=0.7,
    max_tokens=1000,
    api_key="sk-..."  # Or use environment variable  # pragma: allowlist secret
)

docs.title: ProcessingSpec
docs.text:
from ondine.core.specifications import ProcessingSpec

spec = ProcessingSpec(
    batch_size=100,
    concurrency=5,
    max_retries=3,
    checkpoint_interval=500,
    rate_limit=60  # requests per minute
)

docs.title: Synchronous (Default)
docs.text:
Sequential, single-threaded. Simplest to reason about:

result = pipeline.execute()

Good when the dataset fits in memory and you want predictable behavior.

docs.title: Asynchronous
docs.text:
Concurrent processing with async/await:

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    ...
    .with_async_execution(max_concurrency=10)
    .build()
)

result = await pipeline.execute_async()

Best when you need high throughput and the provider API supports concurrent connections.

docs.title: Streaming
docs.text:
Memory-efficient processing for large datasets:

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    ...
    .with_streaming(chunk_size=1000)
    .build()
)

result = pipeline.execute()

Use this for datasets north of 100K rows or when memory is tight. See the Execution Modes guide for a full comparison.

docs.title: Adapters
docs.text: Adapters wrap external dependencies behind stable interfaces.

docs.title: LLM Client
docs.text:
All providers share a single calling convention:

from ondine.adapters import LLMClient

# Automatically selected based on provider
client = create_llm_client(llm_spec)
response = client.complete(prompt, temperature=0.7)

Supported providers: OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Anthropic Claude, Groq, MLX (local on Apple Silicon), and any OpenAI-compatible API.

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
Checkpoint persistence:

from ondine.adapters import CheckpointStorage

storage = CheckpointStorage(path="./checkpoints")
storage.save(state)
state = storage.load()

docs.title: Data IO
docs.text:
Reads and writes CSV, Parquet, Excel, and JSON:

from ondine.adapters import DataIO

# Supports CSV, Parquet, Excel, JSON
data = DataIO.read("data.csv")
DataIO.write(data, "output.parquet")

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text: When you call pipeline.execute(), Ondine first validates configuration and input data, then calculates an expected cost estimate. It checks for an existing checkpoint and resumes from there if one exists. Data is loaded (streaming or in-memory), prompts are formatted with input variables, and the LLM is called with rate limiting and retries. Responses are parsed and validated, results are written to the output destination, and metrics (costs, tokens, timing) are aggregated. On success, the checkpoint is cleaned up.

docs.title: Automatic Retries
docs.text:
Failed requests retry with exponential backoff:

.with_retry_policy(
    max_retries=3,
    backoff_factor=2.0,
    retry_on=[RateLimitError, NetworkError]
)

docs.title: Checkpointing
docs.text:
Resume long-running jobs after a failure:

.with_checkpoint("./checkpoints", interval=100)

docs.title: Error Policies
docs.text:
Control how errors are handled:

.with_error_policy("continue")  # Continue on errors
.with_error_policy("stop")      # Stop on first error

docs.title: Cost Tracking
docs.text:
Costs are tracked in real-time as requests complete:

result = pipeline.execute()

print(f"Total cost: ${result.costs.total_cost:.4f}")
print(f"Input tokens: {result.costs.input_tokens}")
print(f"Output tokens: {result.costs.output_tokens}")
print(f"Cost per row: ${result.costs.total_cost / result.metrics.processed_rows:.6f}")

docs.title: Budget Control
docs.text:
Hard-cap your spend so a runaway pipeline stops before it drains your account:

from decimal import Decimal

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    ...
    .with_max_budget(Decimal("10.0"))  # Max $10 USD
    .build()
)

# Execution stops if budget exceeded
result = pipeline.execute()

docs.title: Progress Bars
docs.text:
tqdm tracks progress automatically:

Processing: 100%|████████| 1000/1000 [00:45<00:00, 22.1rows/s]

docs.title: Structured Logging
docs.text:
JSON-formatted output via structlog:

from ondine.utils import configure_logging

configure_logging(level="INFO", json_format=True)

docs.title: Metrics Export
docs.text:
Push metrics to Prometheus:

from ondine.utils import MetricsExporter

exporter = MetricsExporter(port=9090)
exporter.start()

docs.text:

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10 or higher
  • pip or uv package manager

docs.title: Using pip
docs.text: pip install ondine

docs.title: Using uv (Recommended)
docs.text:
uv resolves and installs packages significantly faster than pip:

# Install uv if you don't have it
curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh

# Install ondine
uv pip install ondine

docs.title: Optional Dependencies
docs.text: Install only what you need. Each extra pulls in a focused set of packages.

docs.title: MLX (Apple Silicon Local Inference)
docs.text:
Run models locally on Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) with MLX:

pip install ondine[mlx]

Requirements: - macOS with Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3/M4) - 16GB+ RAM recommended

Supported models: - Qwen-2.5 series - Llama models - Mistral models - Any MLX-compatible model from Hugging Face

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
OpenTelemetry-based tracing and metrics:

pip install ondine[observability]

Adds distributed tracing (Jaeger), custom metrics export, and performance monitoring.

docs.title: Development Tools
docs.text:
For contributing to Ondine:

pip install ondine[dev]

Pulls in pytest, ruff, mypy, pre-commit hooks, and security scanners (bandit, pip-audit).

docs.title: Redis (Response Caching)
docs.text:
LiteLLM Redis-backed response caching:

pip install ondine[redis]

Installs: redis>=5.0.0

docs.title: Excel Support
docs.text:
Read and write .xlsx / .xls files:

pip install ondine[excel]

Installs: openpyxl>=3.1.0

docs.title: Parquet Support
docs.text:
Read and write Parquet files:

pip install ondine[parquet]

Installs: pyarrow>=15.0.0

docs.title: TUI (Terminal User Interface)
docs.text:
Interactive terminal dashboard:

pip install ondine[tui]

Installs: textual>=1.0.0

docs.title: Performance (uvloop)
docs.text:
Faster async I/O on Linux and macOS:

pip install ondine[performance]

Installs: uvloop>=0.19.0 (not installed on Windows)

docs.title: Knowledge Base / RAG
docs.text:
PDF ingestion and embedding-based knowledge stores:

pip install ondine[knowledge]

Installs: pymupdf>=1.24, sentence-transformers>=3.0

docs.title: Zep (Long-Term Memory)
docs.text:
Zep Cloud-backed memory and conversation history:

pip install ondine[zep]

Installs: zep-cloud>=2.0

docs.title: Azure (Managed Identity)
docs.text:
Required for Azure OpenAI with Managed Identity authentication:

pip install ondine[azure]

Installs: azure-identity>=1.15.0

See Azure Managed Identity Guide for setup details.

docs.title: Install All Optional Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install ondine[all]

[all] installs every optional extra except [dev], [observability], [docs], and [mlx]. Install those separately as needed:

pip install ondine[all,mlx,observability]

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
import ondine

print(ondine.__version__)
print("Ondine installed successfully!")

Or use the CLI:

ondine --version

docs.title: API Keys Setup
docs.text: Set provider keys as environment variables:

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text: export OPENAI_API_KEY="sk-..."  # pragma: allowlist secret

docs.title: Azure OpenAI
docs.text:
Option 1: Managed Identity (Recommended for Production)

# Install Azure dependencies
pip install ondine[azure]

# For local development, login with Azure CLI
az login

# No API keys needed! Uses Managed Identity automatically.

Option 2: API Key (Traditional)

export AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY="..."  # pragma: allowlist secret
export AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT="https://..."
export AZURE_OPENAI_API_VERSION="2024-02-15-preview"

See Azure Managed Identity Guide for detailed setup.

docs.title: Anthropic Claude
docs.text: export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-..."  # pragma: allowlist secret

docs.title: Groq
docs.text: export GROQ_API_KEY="gsk_..."  # pragma: allowlist secret

docs.title: Environment File
docs.text:
Or put everything in a .env file at your project root:

# .env
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...
ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=sk-ant-...
GROQ_API_KEY=gsk_...

Ondine loads .env files automatically via python-dotenv.

docs.title: Upgrade
docs.text: pip install --upgrade ondine

docs.title: Import Error: No module named 'ondine'
docs.text:
Verify you're in the right Python environment:

python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)"
pip list | grep ondine

docs.title: MLX Installation Issues
docs.text: MLX only works on Apple Silicon. If imports fail, check three things: uname -m should print arm64, Xcode Command Line Tools must be installed (xcode-select --install), and a clean reinstall often fixes stale state: pip uninstall mlx mlx-lm && pip install ondine[mlx].

docs.title: API Key Not Found
docs.text: Authentication errors usually mean the key isn't in scope. Run echo $OPENAI_API_KEY to confirm the variable is set, check that your .env file is in the project root, and restart your Python session after any changes.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Build your first Ondine pipeline in under five minutes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • Ondine installed (pip install ondine)
  • An API key for OpenAI or another LLM provider

docs.title: 1. Setup
docs.text:
Create a new Python file and set your API key:

import os
os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = "sk-..."  # Or use .env file

docs.title: 2. Prepare Sample Data
docs.text:
Create a simple CSV file or use a pandas DataFrame:

import pandas as pd

# Sample data
data = pd.DataFrame({
    "product": [
        "iPhone 15 Pro Max 256GB",
        "Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra",
        "Google Pixel 8 Pro"
    ]
})

# Save to CSV
data.to_csv("products.csv", index=False)

docs.title: 3. Quick API (Simplest)
docs.text:
Fewest lines of code to get results:

from ondine import QuickPipeline

# Create and run pipeline
pipeline = QuickPipeline.create(
    data="products.csv",
    prompt="Extract the brand name from: {product}",
    model="gpt-4o-mini"
)

result = pipeline.execute()

# View results
print(result.data)
print(f"Cost: ${result.costs.total_cost:.4f}")

Output: 

   product                      response
0  iPhone 15 Pro Max 256GB       Apple
1  Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra      Samsung
2  Google Pixel 8 Pro            Google

Cost: $0.0012

docs.title: 4. Builder API (More Control)
docs.text:
When you need to pin temperature, set concurrency, or name your output columns explicitly:

from ondine import PipelineBuilder

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_csv(
        "products.csv",
        input_columns=["product"],
        output_columns=["brand"]
    )
    .with_prompt("Extract the brand name from: {product}")
    .with_llm(
        provider="openai",
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        temperature=0.0
    )
    .with_batch_size(100)
    .with_concurrency(5)
    .build()
)

# Estimate cost before running
estimate = pipeline.estimate_cost()
print(f"Estimated cost: ${estimate.total_cost:.4f}")

# Execute
result = pipeline.execute()
print(result.data)

docs.title: Pattern 1: Data Cleaning
docs.text:
from ondine import QuickPipeline

pipeline = QuickPipeline.create(
    data="messy_data.csv",
    prompt="""
    Clean and standardize this text:
    {text}

    Remove special characters, fix capitalization, trim whitespace.
    """,
    model="gpt-4o-mini"
)

result = pipeline.execute()

docs.title: Pattern 2: Classification
docs.text:
from ondine import PipelineBuilder

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_csv(
        "reviews.csv",
        input_columns=["review_text"],
        output_columns=["sentiment"]
    )
    .with_prompt("""
    Classify the sentiment of this review as: positive, negative, or neutral

    Review: {review_text}
    """)
    .with_llm(provider="openai", model="gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0.0)
    .build()
)

result = pipeline.execute()

docs.title: Pattern 3: Structured Extraction
docs.text:
from ondine import PipelineBuilder
from ondine.stages.parser_factory import JSONParser

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_csv(
        "descriptions.csv",
        input_columns=["description"],
        output_columns=["brand", "model", "price"]
    )
    .with_prompt("""
    Extract product information as JSON:
    {{
      "brand": "...",
      "model": "...",
      "price": "..."
    }}

    Description: {description}
    """)
    .with_llm(provider="openai", model="gpt-4o-mini")
    .with_parser(JSONParser())
    .build()
)

result = pipeline.execute()

docs.title: Understanding the Results
docs.text:
execute() returns an ExecutionResult with the processed DataFrame, metrics, and cost breakdown:

result = pipeline.execute()

# Access the processed data
print(result.data)              # pandas DataFrame with results

# View metrics
print(result.metrics.processed_rows)    # Number of rows processed
print(result.metrics.successful_rows)   # Successfully processed
print(result.metrics.failed_rows)       # Failed rows
print(result.metrics.elapsed_time)      # Total time in seconds

# Check costs
print(result.costs.total_cost)          # Total cost in USD
print(result.costs.input_tokens)        # Input tokens used
print(result.costs.output_tokens)       # Output tokens generated

docs.title: Cost Estimation
docs.text:
Estimate costs before processing large datasets. A 100k-row job on GPT-4o can run into real money; five seconds of estimation saves you from a surprise invoice:

pipeline = PipelineBuilder.create()...build()

# Get cost estimate
estimate = pipeline.estimate_cost()

print(f"Estimated rows: {estimate.total_rows}")
print(f"Estimated tokens: {estimate.estimated_tokens}")
print(f"Estimated cost: ${estimate.total_cost:.4f}")

# Proceed only if acceptable
if estimate.total_cost < 10.0:
    result = pipeline.execute()
else:
    print("Cost too high, aborting")

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
Failed requests are retried automatically with exponential backoff:

from ondine import PipelineBuilder

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_csv("data.csv", ...)
    .with_prompt("...")
    .with_llm(provider="openai", model="gpt-4o-mini")
    .with_retry_policy(max_retries=3, backoff_factor=2.0)
    .build()
)

result = pipeline.execute()

# Check for failures
if result.metrics.failed_rows > 0:
    print(f"Failed rows: {result.metrics.failed_rows}")
    print(result.data[result.data['response'].isna()])

docs.title: Checkpointing
docs.text:
Long-running jobs should checkpoint so a crash at row 9,500 of 10,000 doesn't force a full restart:

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_csv("large_dataset.csv", ...)
    .with_prompt("...")
    .with_llm(provider="openai", model="gpt-4o-mini")
    .with_checkpoint("./checkpoints", interval=100)  # Save every 100 rows
    .build()
)

# If interrupted, re-run same command - it will resume from checkpoint
result = pipeline.execute()

docs.title: Azure Managed Identity Authentication
docs.text: Complete guide for using Azure Managed Identity with Ondine for keyless, secure authentication.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Azure Managed Identity provides keyless authentication to Azure OpenAI Service, eliminating the need to store API keys in your code or environment variables. This is the recommended approach for production deployments on Azure.

docs.title: Benefits
docs.text:
• 🔒 No secrets in code - Zero API keys to manage
  • 🔄 Automatic rotation - Azure AD handles credential lifecycle
  • 🎯 Fine-grained access - Use Azure RBAC for permissions
  • 📊 Audit trail - All access logged through Azure AD
  • 🚀 Works everywhere - Production, staging, and local development

docs.title: 1. Install Azure Dependencies
docs.text:
pip install ondine[azure]

This installs azure-identity>=1.15.0 for Managed Identity support.

docs.title: 2. Configure Azure Resources
docs.text:
# Assign Managed Identity to your Azure resource
az vm identity assign --name my-vm --resource-group my-rg

# Grant "Cognitive Services OpenAI User" role
az role assignment create \
  --assignee <managed-identity-principal-id> \
  --role "Cognitive Services OpenAI User" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/<openai-resource>

docs.title: 3. Use in Your Code
docs.text:
from ondine import PipelineBuilder

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_csv("data.csv", input_columns=["text"], output_columns=["result"])
    .with_prompt("Process: {text}")
    .with_llm(
        provider="azure_openai",
        model="gpt-4",
        azure_endpoint="https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/",
        azure_deployment="gpt-4-deployment",
        use_managed_identity=True  # ← No API key needed!
    )
    .build()
)

result = pipeline.execute()

docs.title: Example 1: Basic Usage
docs.text:
from ondine import PipelineBuilder
import pandas as pd

# Sample data
data = pd.DataFrame({
    "product_description": [
        "Wireless Bluetooth headphones with noise cancellation",
        "Stainless steel water bottle, 32oz capacity",
    ]
})

# Build pipeline with Managed Identity
pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_dataframe(
        data,
        input_columns=["product_description"],
        output_columns=["category", "features"]
    )
    .with_prompt("""
Analyze: {product_description}

Provide:
1. category: Product category
2. features: Key features (comma-separated)

Format as JSON: {{"category": "...", "features": "..."}}
""")
    .with_llm(
        provider="azure_openai",
        model="gpt-4",
        azure_endpoint="https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/",
        azure_deployment="gpt-4-deployment",
        use_managed_identity=True,
        temperature=0.7,
    )
    .with_batch_size(10)
    .with_concurrency(5)
    .build()
)

result = pipeline.execute()
print(f"Processed: {result.metrics.total_rows} rows")
print(f"Cost: ${result.costs.total_cost:.4f}")

docs.title: Example 2: Environment-Aware Configuration
docs.text:
import os
from ondine import PipelineBuilder

# Detect if running on Azure
is_azure = os.getenv("WEBSITE_INSTANCE_ID") or os.getenv("MSI_ENDPOINT")

builder = PipelineBuilder.create().from_csv("data.csv", ...)

if is_azure:
    # Production: Use Managed Identity
    builder.with_llm(
        provider="azure_openai",
        model="gpt-4",
        azure_endpoint="https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/",
        azure_deployment="gpt-4-deployment",
        use_managed_identity=True
    )
else:
    # Development: Use API Key
    builder.with_llm(
        provider="azure_openai",
        model="gpt-4",
        azure_endpoint="https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/",
        azure_deployment="gpt-4-deployment"
        # Falls back to AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY env var
    )

pipeline = builder.build()

docs.title: Example 1: Managed Identity Config
docs.text:
Create azure_managed_identity.yaml:

name: "product_enrichment_pipeline"
version: "1.0"

dataset:
  source_type: "csv"
  source_path: "products.csv"
  input_columns:
    - "product_description"
  output_columns:
    - "category"
    - "key_features"

prompt:
  template: "Analyze this product: {product_description}\n\nProvide category and key features as JSON."
  response_format: "json"

llm:
  provider: "azure_openai"
  model: "gpt-4"
  azure_endpoint: "https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/"
  azure_deployment: "gpt-4-deployment"
  use_managed_identity: true  # ← Keyless authentication!
  temperature: 0.7

processing:
  batch_size: 50
  concurrency: 10

output:
  destination_type: "csv"
  destination_path: "enriched_products.csv"

Run with CLI:

ondine process --config azure_managed_identity.yaml

docs.title: Example 2: API Key Config (Traditional)

Create azure_api_key.yaml:

name: "support_ticket_classification"
version: "1.0"

  source_type: "csv"
  source_path: "tickets.csv"
  input_columns:
    - "ticket_description"
  output_columns:
    - "category"

  template: "Classify: {ticket_description}"

llm:
  provider: "azure_openai"
  model: "gpt-4"
  azure_endpoint: "https://your-resource.openai.azure.com/"
  azure_deployment: "gpt-4-deployment"
  # No use_managed_identity → falls back to AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY

processing:
  batch_size: 100

output:
  destination_type: "csv"
  destination_path: "classified_tickets.csv"

Run with CLI:

export AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key-here"  # pragma: allowlist secret
ondine process --config azure_api_key.yaml

docs.title: Multi-Region Deployment
docs.text:
For global deployments, use region-specific endpoints:

import os

# Define region-specific endpoints
AZURE_REGIONS = {
    "eastus": "https://eastus-openai.openai.azure.com/",
    "westeurope": "https://westeurope-openai.openai.azure.com/",
    "swedencentral": "https://swedencentral-openai.openai.azure.com/",
    "japaneast": "https://japaneast-openai.openai.azure.com/",
}

# Get region from environment
region = os.getenv("AZURE_REGION", "eastus")
endpoint = AZURE_REGIONS[region]

pipeline = (
    PipelineBuilder.create()
    .from_csv("data.csv", ...)
    .with_llm(
        provider="azure_openai",
        model="gpt-4",
        azure_endpoint=endpoint,  # Region-specific
        azure_deployment="gpt-4-deployment",
        use_managed_identity=True
    )
    .build()
)

docs.title: Error: "azure-identity not installed"

'Cognitive Services OpenAI User' role.

Solutions:

  1. Check Managed Identity is assigned:

az account show  # Verify you're logged in

docs.title: Error: "No authentication provided"

ValueError: Azure OpenAI requires either:
  1. use_managed_identity=True (for keyless auth), or
  2. api_key parameter, or
  3. AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable

Solution: Choose one authentication method:

# Option 1: Managed Identity
.with_llm(..., use_managed_identity=True)

# Option 2: API Key  # pragma: allowlist secret

# Option 3: Environment variable
export AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY="your-key"  # pragma: allowlist secret

docs.title: ✅ Do's
docs.text:
1. Use Managed Identity in production - No secrets to manage
  2. Use az login for local development - Personal credentials
  3. Grant minimum required RBAC roles - Principle of least privilege
  4. Use separate identities per environment - Dev, staging, prod
  5. Monitor Azure AD logs - Track authentication events

docs.title: ❌ Don'ts
docs.text:
1. Don't hardcode API keys - Use Managed Identity instead
  2. Don't commit credentials - Never in source control
  3. Don't use API keys in production - Use Managed Identity
  4. Don't share Managed Identities - One per application
  5. Don't skip RBAC - Always use role-based access control

Aspect API Key Managed Identity Security ⚠️ Secret to manage ✅ No secrets Rotation ❌ Manual ✅ Automatic Setup ✅ Simple ⚠️ Requires Azure setup Local Dev ✅ Easy (env var) ✅ Easy (az login) Production ❌ Risk of exposure ✅ Secure by design Audit Trail ⚠️ Limited ✅ Full Azure AD logs RBAC ❌ No ✅ Yes Cost Free Free

Recommendation: Use Managed Identity for production, API key for quick prototyping.
